items.name: Knight
items.description: A moderate-damage single-target melee troop. The classic mini-tank. Despite only targeting ground, this jack-of-most-trades card can act as a great mini-tank and a fair defense against small troops with his moderate-high hitpoints and decent damage output. All for only 3 Elixir; good stats for his cost if you ask anyone!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Knight gets talked about like the definition of “boring but never sells.” People rate him as insanely consistent for the cost—cheap mini-tank that just soaks hits, distracts forever, and patches defense in basically any deck. There’s also a split where some see base Knight as outclassed (since other options/variants get more hype), but even then it’s more “not exciting” than actually bad.

items.name: Archers
items.description: Two low-damage yet medium-ranged single-target troops with knowledge in hair coloring. Targeting both ground and air, and lightly armored, they are inexpensive support troops or a quick defense against small pushes while costing only 3 Elixir. They're weak to decent splash damage, though.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Archers are one of those cards people either call completely useless or quietly rely on because they’re just consistent, cheap ranged DPS that covers air. A big chunk of the community vibe is that base Archers feel like they do “no damage” and don’t threaten anything unless you’re basically playing them to cycle (or you’re really talking about the EVO). At the same time, plenty of players still see them as a steady, never-terrible staple in a couple classic control/cycle shells (like X-Bow), even if they’re not taking over the meta.

items.name: Goblins
items.description: Four very fast, moderate damage, single-target melee troops. Unarmored and only targeting ground, they are nevertheless an excellent defense against glass cannons like the Mini P.E.K.K.A., and not to be ignored with a tank or mini-tank. All for only 2 Elixir! Just watch out for any gold that you might have.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblins get talked about like the definition of “fine but why?”—they do their job as a cheap stabby cycle/distraction card, but a lot of players feel they’re just the boring option. The big knock is that Goblin Gang basically powercreeps them for +1 elixir, so standalone Goblins feel kind of pointless unless you’re hard-minmaxing cycle. Overall vibe: usable, not exciting, and usually replaced by a better package.

items.name: Giant
items.description: The "original" tank, the Giant is a 5 Elixir meat shield with high health and moderate melee damage. He ignores all enemy troops and slowly makes his way towards buildings, soaking up damage to protect any troops behind him. Once he gets there, he’ll start breaking it! A real one-man wrecking crew! If hisHero Form'is active, don't get near him, because for 2 Elixir, you can use his:Heroic Hurlability, making the Giant kick the highest health troop to the air and by a long distance, dealing very small impact damage. Best not to anger the Giant!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Giant gets talked about like the classic, reliable tank win con that just always has a place—big stats, simple gameplan, and it doesn’t instantly lose you the match for dropping it. People see it as less of a “coin-flip” commitment than heavier beatdown (since you can still defend after Giant in the back), and it’s generally viewed as a solid, fair card rather than some cringe no-skill gimmick. Overall vibe: timeless and strong across most of ladder, even if it’s not always the absolute #1 meta bully.

items.name: P.E.K.K.A
items.description: A very tender robot from the inside, unless you decide to be mean to her or her butterflies! She has extremely high damage and hitpoints but is very slow and easy to distract with swarms, often requiring splash support. For 7 Elixir and her very high stats, she’s very reliable for defense & tanking, and can devastate towers — if she gets there.
items.llm_vibe_summary: P.E.K.K.A gets talked about like she’s all aura, but in practice she’s a pricey brick that gets clowned by cheap distractions (skeletons/ice spirit type stuff) and spends half the game waddling into kites. When she actually connects, it’s basically lights-out, but that’s the problem—getting a real tower lock feels rare and super matchup-dependent. Overall the vibe is “looks scary, plays mid,” and a lot of people would rather spend that elixir on something less easy to stall.

items.name: Minions
items.description: Three fast, moderate-damage single-target ranged air troops. Unarmored but targeting both ground and air, they can be a high-damage support card or a quick defense against glass cannons at the cost of 3 Elixir. A bit vulnerable to splash damage, but it'll do the trick.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Minions get talked about like the ultimate plug-and-play air DPS: cheap, versatile, and they shred everything if you don’t have the answer right now. One side of the community basically calls them absurdly strong and everywhere, like they do every job you’d want from a 3-elixir air card. The other vibe is more “they’re fine, a bit out of meta,” so the perception splits between ‘lowkey broken’ and ‘just solid’ depending on what you’re seeing on ladder.

items.name: Balloon
items.description: Watch out, an air raid is coming! Having decent hitpoints and high damage per strike, while only targeting buildings, the Balloon makes for a heavy threat on offense. Although it isn’t fast, one strike could potentially mean defeat. For 5 Elixir, it brings death from above and can even blow up a low-health Crown Tower and smaller support troops with its death damage as a last-ditch effort.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Balloon is seen as a super swingy win con: some matchups you just farm towers, and other matchups you’re staring at Inferno Tower/Archers/Minions and you’re never breaking through. People still rate it pretty high because it’s versatile across multiple decks (Loon cycle, LavaLoon, LumberLoon), but it’s not at its “peak” and doesn’t feel consistent. Overall vibe is basically matchup/slot-machine Clash: when it works it’s disgusting, when it doesn’t you feel useless.

items.name: Witch
items.description: While her health is only decent and splash damage is mediocre for 5 Elixir, she summons 4 Skeletons every 7 seconds to help distract & destroy, making her useful for defense and support. However, she does take a short while to begin summoning them, and she’s especially weak against other splash-damage dealers which easily crush her Skeletons and shrug off her destructo beams, leaving her defenseless.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Witch is the kind of card people call “mad annoying” because it’s constant skeleton spam plus splash, so it stalls forever and makes defense feel gross even when it’s not doing much on offense. There’s a split in how people rate it: some see base Witch as mid and not really meta, while others treat it like an insane defensive staple (especially when you factor in how busted the evolved version feels). Overall the vibe is: irritating, value-y on defense, and way more respected/feared when EVO is in the conversation.

items.name: Barbarians
items.description: Five single-target melee troops with thoroughly average stats. Despite only targeting ground, they are a mighty collective force and can swiftly deal with a great variety of threats for 5 Elixir, but keep them away from powerful splash cards.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Barbarians get talked about like a 5-elixir overcommit that just begs to be Fireballed or farmed by any splash in the game, so they rarely feel worth the slot. People admit you can cook up a “value” scenario sometimes, but most of the time they’re just spellable, clunky, and you’d rather run a cheaper swarm/defender that doesn’t instantly donate elixir.

items.name: Golem
items.description: A colossal rock juggernaut. Not only are his massive hitpoints the highest of any unit, he deals great damage and only targets buildings. Upon death, he explodes, splitting into 2 Golemites (weaker, but still tanks) while dealing instant death damage to everything near him. Although he costs a hefty 8 Elixir, he moves so slowly that he can be supported like he costed a Giant's worth of Elixir!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Golem gets talked about like a slot-machine win condition: you drop it and either you steamroll because they don’t have the right answers, or you instantly get punished and feel cooked. People respect it more than the truly brain-dead stuff because it’s expensive and you can’t just spam it—one bad Golem and you lose a tower. At the same time, it’s still seen as a polarizing beatdown card that can just roll less-prepared players, especially outside the very top end.

items.name: Skeletons
items.description: Three fast, low-damage single-target melee troops. They only target ground and are completely unarmored, but are very useful. They can distract enemies when timed right or just exist to cycle your deck for the dirt-cheap price of 1 Elixir! Think about the amount of value!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Skeletons are basically the definition of “never bad” — cheap, always useful, and they bail you out on defense by distracting and buying time for almost no elixir. People talk about them like the best pure cycle card in the game: they fit everywhere, enable outplays, and don’t feel like a “gimmick” slot. The only knock is they’re still just skeletons, so they’re not flashy — they’re value and fundamentals, not highlight reels.

items.name: Valkyrie
items.description: A 4 Elixir powerhouse with high health. This tough mini-tank swings her axe 360º to take out swarms and support troops alike. Despite this, she can't reach air troops and her slow movement speed leaves her susceptible to ranged attacks.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Valkyrie gets talked about like the ultimate plug-and-play mini tank: cheap-ish, stupidly consistent, and she just spins through bait/swarm like it’s nothing. People rate her as one of those cards you can toss into almost any deck and she’ll still do her job, which makes her feel kinda unfair/annoying when you’re trying to get chip going. Not really a flashy “broken” card vibe—more like a timeless defensive god that always seems to get value.

items.name: Skeleton Army
items.description: Fifteen skeletons for just 3 Elixir — this massive swarm can overwhelm single-target enemy units in the blink of an eye! However, with their very low hitpoints, they can be easily taken out by area damage or even several ranged units, so they’re best used on defense with caution. However, you should NOT mock the General of the army, because
items.llm_vibe_summary: Skeleton Army gets talked about like the classic “looks scary, does nothing” card: you drop 3 elixir and it just evaporates to literally any small spell or random splash, so it feels like an overcommit most of the time. One side still acknowledges it can work as a surprise/bait piece, but the dominant vibe is that it’s way too spellable and inconsistent compared to just running Skeletons/Guards. People mostly respect it only when it’s part of evo-driven nonsense—base Skarmy is treated like a relic that sells you on panic value and then loses you the game.

items.name: Bomber
items.description: A 2 Elixir high-damage and medium-ranged splash troop. While fragile and only targeting ground, he is decent against medium to large swarms and can deal great damage behind a tank for his price.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Bomber gets talked about like a fallen-off support troop: it used to be a legit cheap splash option, but now it just feels squishy and kind of irrelevant, dying to everything and rarely swinging games. The vibe is basically “why would I run this when better splash/cycle exists,” so it comes off like a dead card most of the time. Some people still call it situationally useful, but it’s usually framed as underwhelming value unless the matchup is perfect.

items.name: Musketeer
items.description: A consistent 4 Elixir single-target shot attacking both air and ground with fairly average stats — she is a reliable support or defense unit with a trusty boomstick that deals enough damage to take out small tanks but enough speed to quickly handle small swarms, though she's vulnerable to spells.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Musketeer gets talked about like the “default” ranged unit that just does her job every game—solid DPS, reliable anti-air, and way less gimmicky than most options. People rate her as more consistent and sturdy than stuff like Magic Archer, so she ends up feeling like a safe, respectable pick in a ton of decks. She’s not really a hype or toxic card—more like a dependable meta staple that quietly wins you defenses and converts into counterpushes.

items.name: Baby Dragon
items.description: A 4 Elixir troop with moderately high hitpoints and fast movement speed that deals low splash damage making him effective as a support troop, killing swarms while chipping at ground troops and towers. He's also a decent mini-tank, but weak against high damage units and takes a while to defeat enemy units.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Baby Dragon gets talked about like the quiet, reliable splash flier you can toss into beatdown/control and it’ll almost always do something useful—people even point out it’s tankier than it looks. There’s also a recurring vibe that the “normal” version is kind of forgotten because everyone associates the card with its EVO and with golem/lava shells. Overall it’s not seen as some toxic brain-dead problem card—more like a solid, slightly slept-on staple that’s good when your deck actually wants that role.

items.name: Prince
items.description: With very high damage and good hitpoints for 5 Elixir, the Prince is powerful on offense and defense. His charge ability gives him extra speed and allows him to deal double damage to a target when he crashes, allowing him to devastate targets or speed up slow units. His trusty pony can also jump over the river, just like a hog. However, his slow attack speed means swarms in particular make it difficult for him to break through.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Prince is that card that’s not always “meta,” but it can absolutely ruin your day when it connects—tons of offensive threat and random charge pressure that forces awkward responses. People talk about it like it’s quietly coming back and showing up more, and when it’s in the right deck it feels really strong instead of just nostalgia. It’s not framed as brain-dead; it’s more like a solid, scary pressure piece that’s annoying as hell when you’re not ready for it.

items.name: Wizard
items.description: For 5 Elixir, the Wizard burns foes on the ground and in the air with heavy-hitting fireballs that deal splash damage. He only has moderate health so protect him to maximize his damage potential. The Wizard excels against groups of enemies, but not so much against tanks, and poetically a Fireball.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Wizard gets talked about like the classic “looks strong but sells every time” card: when he’s splashing your stuff it feels gross, but most games he just dies too easily for 5 elixir and underperforms. People get tilted because he sometimes high-rolls value, yet he’s inconsistent and usually there are better ranged/splash options for the cost. Overall vibe is expensive, squishy, and kind of overrated—annoying when it works, disappointing the rest of the time.

items.name: Mini P.E.K.K.A
items.description: The classic glass cannon — a mighty punch packed powerhouse within a moderate health, for a cost of 4 Elixir. He's a fast single-targeting troop who can delete the majority of support troops or devastate tanks and towers alike with only a few strikes, but is weak against swarms. Deliver him some pancakes, and you'll see his sweet and cute side! If not, then he'll make you. If hisHero Formis active, then all of those months learning from Royal Chef will pay off and he'll begin cooking pancakes for himself! He takes 20 seconds to cook one, but attacking a troop fills the bar by 50%, for a maximum of 5 pancakes (and 5 bars). Then, for 1 Elixir, you can use hisBreakfast Boostability to give him that extra morning energy to get out of the bed, enjoy the day, and level up by how many pancakes he managed to cook! This ability is one-time use only, so make sure to cook pancakes really well, or everything's wasted.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mini P.E.K.K.A gets talked about like a straight-up damage machine: ridiculous single-target DPS for the cost, enough HP to actually matter, and it can swing games when it connects or deletes a tank. A lot of people value it as a reliable, plug-and-play tank killer that doesn’t need fancy setup—just drop it and it does the job. There’s some split perception though: some see the base card as a bit underwhelming compared to flashier modern options, while others still rate it as a consistently strong, honest defensive threat.

items.name: Spear Goblins
items.description: Three very fast, low-damage single-target ranged troops. Unarmored and targeting both ground and air, they are excellent against small single-target ground and air pushes like Minions and are dirt-cheap support units costing only 2 Elixir.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Spear Goblins get talked about like the definition of a low-impact cycle card: tiny damage, tiny distraction time, and you’re usually asking yourself why you didn’t just run Skeletons or Goblin Gang. A lot of players basically treat them as “lame filler” that only shows up in specific cycle/bait lists, so the vibe is that they’re borderline a dead standalone card. There is still some respect for them as a cheap, flexible stab-at-everything option (especially compared to regular Goblins in certain contexts), but it’s more “they’re fine” than “they’re scary.”

items.name: Giant Skeleton
items.description: This oversized skeleton costs 6 Elixir gets distracted by everything, and struggles heavily against swarms or equally bulky units. These weaknesses are made up for with his bomb that explodes three seconds after his death, dealing high damage in its radius. As a cherry on top, it deals double damage to opposing Crown Towers, making it extra threatening! Try to bait the opponent into overcommitting on their defense against it to reap massive positive Elixir trades!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Giant Skeleton is one of those “either it hard carries or it does nothing” cards—when it works, it lane-stops and the bomb/fist value feels disgusting, but it’s still a pretty gimmicky 6-elixir commitment. One side of the community sees it as quietly revived/underrated lately (especially as a lane-stopper in certain meta shells), while the other basically treats it like a washed, distractable, overcosted relic you almost never see. Overall vibe: niche control tank that can feel amazing in the right deck, but looks dead and clunky the moment the matchup or counters are wrong.

items.name: Hog Rider
items.description: A reliable card against towers. Zoom past troops with his moderate-high health and blazing fast speed and landing a hit or two on towers while letting out his signature scream "HOG RIDER!" — all for a measley Elixir. Also, his hog will jump over the river! However, good building placements easily draw him away, and he is seriously helpless against swarms and tank busters.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Hog Rider gets talked about like the definition of a “timeless” win con: you can slap it into a ton of decks, build a whole cycle shell around it, and it just keeps working. People also hate how it always seems to sneak a hit at 1 HP, so it’s consistently annoying even when it’s not the #1 meta deck. Sentiment splits a bit: it’s respected for versatility and consistency, but it also gets side-eyed as a tryhard/cycle spam card that can feel low-effort when you’re the one defending it all game.

items.name: Minion Horde
items.description: Gulp! A horde of 6 Minions for only 5 Elixir! This card has ridiculously high damage per second, allowing it to defeat tanks and pushes in the blink of an eye. But keep caution using them, otherwise you may lose everything... in the blink of an eye.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Minion Horde gets talked about like an old-school menace that just doesn’t survive the modern meta—too many arrows/zap/snowball-style answers and way too much risk for 5 elixir. When it works it can still shred and feel “okay,” but most of the time it’s a value donation that disappears instantly to spells or splash. Overall vibe is: super punishable, super matchup-dependent, and not worth the headache unless you’re really banking on spell-baiting someone.

items.name: Ice Wizard
items.description: Chill out! This Wizard has moderate hitpoints, deals low area damage that slows units and spawns with frost around him that slows enemies (and also deals tiny damage to boot). For 3 Elixir. It may not seem like much, but he can stop enemies in their tracks, making him an nice support troop. Although his slow attack rate and low damage make him ineffective alone, so this chill pill is best used as support.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Ice Wizard gets talked about like a straight-up defensive cheat code: you drop him and big pushes just stop moving, and suddenly you’ve bought a ton of time for cheap. People see him as a top-ladder “slow everything down” staple—annoying for the attacker, but in a respectable, control-player way rather than brain-dead cheese. Overall vibe is consistent and reliable: not flashy, just always doing work when you need to stabilize.

items.name: Royal Giant
items.description: An infamous, hulking beast of a man. Costs 6 Elixir for a great single-target damage to buildings. High in health with somewhat good range, his powerful cannonballs plough through any building he decides to shoot. He's vulnerable to high damage dealers, but he doesn't care. He'll still land a few smacks anyways.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Royal Giant gets talked about like the poster-child of “drop it at the bridge and collect damage” — people genuinely call it zero-skill/brain-dead and get heated about RG players. Even when it’s not the #1 win con at the very top, it’s still seen as consistently strong across most of ladder, which makes losing to it feel extra gross. The vibe is basically: it’s effective, it’s everywhere, and it’s annoying enough that it tilts people on sight.

items.name: Guards
items.description: Surround enemies with this armored pile of bones — 3 Elixir guards with shields that make them incredibly resistant to spells! The worst a Rocket can do is knock their shields off, making them a useful choice for defending most ground troops. Their spears are longer than most other melees, by the way.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Guards get talked about like the "skeletons that don’t get spelled"—they’re insanely reliable on defense because they don’t just evaporate to Log/Zap/Arrows, so you actually get value instead of getting farmed. People see them as one of the best cheap defensive staples in the game, able to clamp down on a ton of threats for the cost. Overall vibe is very positive: not flashy, just consistently nasty and hard to punish.

items.name: Princess
items.description: Royalty steps into the Arena! For 3 Elixir, she sets anything on fire with her moderate hitpoints, area damage, and very long range! She easily defeats any swarm, and can outrange buildings; even your opponent's Crown Towers with flair! If you're feeling something towards her, you were probably hit by her flaming arrows. However, she is fragile and slow to attack — her dreams are easily crushed (along with herself).
items.llm_vibe_summary: Princess is that classic bait staple that never really dies—she’s basically always around because log bait is always around. People talk about her like a 3-elixir value machine: if you don’t answer her, she just chips forever and splashes down swarms from a mile away. She’s not framed as “brain-dead,” but she is absolutely the kind of card that feels annoying and oppressive when she’s getting free shots and forcing you to spend spells awkwardly.

items.name: Dark Prince
items.description: For 4 Elixir, the lesser known Prince has moderately high hitpoints, and a shield to absorb any hit. When attacking, he has an ordinary melee splash that hits in front, and like his cousin, the Prince, a charge attack that's just like his regular attack albeit dealing double damage alongside a pony that can jump over the river. He is flexible for both defense and counter-attacks, but is vulnerable to high damage units.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Dark Prince gets talked about like a sneaky strong glue card: splash, shield value, and real offensive pressure for a “mini tank” slot. One side of the community is basically shocked he’s statistically cooking right now and treats him as genuinely meta-strong; the other side is more lukewarm and never really bought into the hype. Net vibe is that he’s not flashy, but he quietly wins interactions and feels better than people give him credit for.

items.name: Three Musketeers
items.description: Three independent, royal, very powerful markswomen with high burst damage, moderate hitpoints and long range. They can stop almost anything with their powerful bayonets, attacking slower than the lone Musketeer, but having more power in their little knifes attached to their good old (but improved) boomsticks. However, they're a very heavy investment (9 whopping Elixir!) and they're vulnerable to the heaviest of spells, so watch out for net losses!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Three Musketeers gets talked about like a polarizing “just drop it and win” card: insane range/DPS that can feel brain-dead when it’s online, and it steamrolls people who don’t have the exact spell answers. At the same time, a lot of players call it gimmicky because one well-timed Fireball/Lightning/Poison swing can make the whole 9-elixir investment look stupid. So the vibe is either you’re getting flat-out destroyed by it, or you’re watching it get spelled and thinking “why would anyone run this?”

items.name: Lava Hound
items.description: A majestic flying beast that targets buildings with very high hitpoints but very low damage. When defeated, he splits into 6 spread-out and less majestic Lava Pups that can easily overwhelm units, each dealing larger damage than the Hound himself, and with low health. However, this tank is slow and expensive at 7 Elixir, and must be well-supported.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Lava Hound gets talked about like a straight slot machine win con: you queue up and either they’ve got the air answers and you’re cooked, or they don’t and you can outplay and steal games. People don’t really call it brain-dead—if anything it’s seen as hard to pilot well and kind of miserable when the meta is packed with air counters. Overall vibe is “inconsistent but can pop off,” and it feels way less reliable than it used to be.

items.name: Ice Spirit
items.description: This fast, 1 Elixir little snowball has excellent defensive and offensive capabilities due to his Ice Blast, which can reset channeled abilities, force enemies to retarget, or even freeze the opposition as potent support in a push. His cheap cost also makes him great to quickly shuffle through a player's deck, and he's got enough health to survive one attack from most units while he deploys, allowing him to jump on them. But what if 1 isn't enough? Well,
items.llm_vibe_summary: Ice Spirit gets talked about like the definition of a “never bad” card: cheap, fits basically everywhere, and always finds value on defense or supporting a push. People don’t really call it brain-dead—if you actually know how to time and place it, it hard swings interactions for 1 elixir. Overall it’s seen as a staple cycle piece that’s just always solid.

items.name: Fire Spirit
items.description: One very fast, near-melee splash troop that packs a flaming hot punch for only 1 Elixir! He proves to be a good incinerating tool against tightly-packed swarms and glass cannons, and adds a second layer of offense to even the smallest pushes. However, he can be easily snuffed out due to his low hitpoints, so constantly look for opportunities to sneak him onto enemy troops, preferably melee troops.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Fire Spirit gets talked about like the "meh" spirit: it’s playable and does its job, but it’s not the one people are hyped to run when Ice/Electro/Heal Spirit exist. The vibe is basically “okay, not terrible,” mostly showing up as a filler cycle/splash chip option rather than something that swings matchups. Overall perception is low-drama and low-ceiling—fine, just not the spirit you’re picking first.

items.name: Miner
items.description: This underground mini-tank is always a surprise to the opponent! For 3 Elixir, he has a lot to offer: moderate hitpoints, damage and the ability to go anywhere in the Arena! He can be a quick mini-tank for smaller troops, easily sabotage enemy buildings, or shovel up frail defenders, but watch out for swarms, as he can't do much against them.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Miner gets talked about like the “classy” way to win: slow, methodical chip where you actually have to think, not just spam a bridge button. The vibe is super respectful—losing to Miner control feels fair, like you got outplayed instead of cheesed. At the same time, people admit he’s fallen off a bit and is more of a secondary win con/sniping tool now than the centerpiece of the meta.

items.name: Sparky
items.description: Wait… where did the Red King's push go? For 6 Elixir, Sparky is a powerful machine with high hitpoints that nukes a small area on the ground every 4 seconds, making her a juggernaut on both defense and offense. However, her very slow attack rate allows her to be easily surrounded or distracted. Also, any and all electricity (barring the Tesla) causes her to short-circuit and restart her charge.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Sparky’s community vibe is super split: some people think she’s secretly strong right now and way better than her reputation, especially when she’s protected behind a tank and you don’t have the clean answer. Other players still call her clunky and overpriced because she’s a 6-elixir ground-only cannon that gets reset/zapped, distracted, or denied before she ever cashes out. When she does connect, it feels disgusting and unfair, but a lot of games she’s either a win-more threat or a dead slot depending on matchup and cycle.

items.name: Bowler
items.description: This huge menacing but also relaxed ogre with high hitpoints throws boulders that pierce through ground units in its path! For 5 Elixir, he can crush and knock back glass cannons, like he was in a bowling alley, and is healthy enough to survive even a Rocket. Despite all this, he does have quite a slow attack rate, so he must be supported well.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Bowler gets talked about like a straight-up lane-stopper: you drop him and he just deletes bridge spam, hog-type pressure, and swarms with that knockback splash. People describe him as insanely clutch and satisfying when you’re down bad on elixir and he still stabilizes the whole defense. The vibe is mostly respect—he’s not some cheesy brain-rot win con, he’s just a chunky control piece that quietly carries games when used right.

items.name: Lumberjack
items.description: A fast and powerful Barbarian for 4 Elixir, the Lumberjack provides high damage and speed with moderate hitpoints. He shreds tanks, towers, and even small swarms with his extremely fast attack speed and high damage, making him formidable whether on defense or offense. He also drops a bottle of Rage when he falls, boosting any friendly units nearby!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Lumberjack gets talked about like a solid on-paper DPS support that’s held back by being kinda squishy and not always getting consistent value unless the situation’s right. A lot of the hype is tied to what he enables (rage swings and tempo), but people also see him as pretty deck-dependent—either he’s doing work in specific win-con pairings or he’s just “fine.” Overall vibe is more practical than flashy: not broken, not dead, just a situationally clutch piece that can feel way better when the rest of the deck is built around him.

items.name: Battle Ram
items.description: A fast but fragile 4 Elixir surprise to smash into buildings and disrupt the defense. Once it gets charging, it must be taken out before it rams into a tower, or the target will take double damage! Two Barbarians also pop out after the ram is destroyed or crashed, making this card reliable for both defense and offense.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Battle Ram gets talked about like it’s fine in theory, but super unreliable as a main win con unless you’re basically building your whole deck around it. People keep bringing up how often it just gets stopped by common stuff (log/snowball/buildings/splash), so it barely connects and feels kinda gimmicky. One side rates it as just “decent but needs the EVO to feel great,” while the other thinks it’s straight up under-average outside of classic bridge spam setups.

items.name: Inferno Dragon
items.description: For 4 Elixir, this dragon has moderately high health and high damage. With his infernal blast, he can make crisps out of tanks and towers alike, and can even survive a Lightning! But beware of swarms and stunning electrickery, as he is shockingly hopeless against them.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Inferno Dragon gets talked about like a "sometimes insane, sometimes useless" card: when it locks onto a tank it melts everything, but half the time it just gets kited, distracted, or stalled and you paid 4 elixir for vibes. A lot of players see it as pretty matchup-dependent and easy to neutralize with cheap distractions/cycle, so it doesn’t feel consistently meta-defining. When it does stick and ramp, though, it’s one of those cards that can feel super annoying because your whole push evaporates.

items.name: Ice Golem
items.description: A cheap yet versatile mini-tank, the Ice Golem provides a wide range of uses for only 2 Elixir. He's slow enough for a non-committal opening in the back, useful for kiting & distracting, flexible in a push, and deals slowing death damage that can destroy low-health enemy units.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Ice Golem gets talked about like the definition of a clean, honest cycle mini-tank: cheap, always useful, and it just does its job (kite, tank a bit, slow stuff) without being cringe. People rate it as a super solid “glue” card that bails you out in defense and makes your cycle smoother. It’s not some flashy takeover card, but it’s one of those picks that basically never feels wrong, even if it’s not the absolute best mini-tank in every meta.

items.name: Mega Minion
items.description: A 3 Elixir flying armored gargoyle with below-average health and high damage. What makes him different from other aerial units? He is spell resistant, so not even a lone Fireball can take him out! Plus, his high damage output will allow him to intimidate anyone with his mighty and scary swipes! His only weaknesses? Swarms, ranged units, and cupcakes.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mega Minion gets talked about like a quiet workhorse: cheap, high DPS, and just reliably cleans up on defense while also backing up pushes. The vibe is mostly “underrated value” rather than flashy—people like that it does its job without needing a whole gimmick. The main knock is it can feel slow and not that threatening on offense, so some players label it kinda mid even though it still holds up in a lot of matchups.

items.name: Dart Goblin
items.description: A 3 Elixir card with a fast fire rate, long range, and high speed, making him a great unit for sniping buildings on enemy territory or rush attacks. Despite this, he does have very low hitpoints so be sure to protect him from moderate damage troops, which can defeat him in a few shots.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Dart Goblin is that ridiculously annoying chip/DPS card that you can’t ignore for even a second—if it’s left alive it just machine-guns your tower and farms value. The vibe is super “I hate this card” because it’s fast, long-range, and forces a response every time, especially in bait shells where dealing with him already puts you in a bad spot. Some people hesitate to call it pure no-skill, but the general talk is that it’s oppressive and tilting to face, and it hard-punishes certain matchups (especially swarm-based defenses).

items.name: Goblin Gang
items.description: Six fast though weak Goblins for 3 Elixir. Three have knives for powerful ground melee combat deployed in front, and three have spears for ranged attacks on both ground and air at the rear. Together, they can clear a multitude of units, but be wary of splash cards when using this Goblin Value Pack!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Gang gets talked about like the classic “does everything” bait swarm: cheap, fast, covers air and ground, and just makes defending and counterpushing feel easy. People rate it as a super consistent, plug-and-play staple (especially in bait/control shells) because it forces awkward spells and gives reliable DPS + distraction value. The vibe isn’t usually “broken,” more like timeless and always useful—though it can still be irritating when it’s part of a full bait package.

items.name: Electro Wizard
items.description: ZAP! For just 4 Elixir, he lands with a free zap and continues to zap two opponents at once or deal double damage to a single foe, making him a stunning support troop anywhere. He has moderate health, and is easily defeated by swarms and high health tanks, but you have to admit, he's shockingly awesome. He also creates stuff on breaks. Have you ever asked who created the Zap and Tesla?
items.llm_vibe_summary: Electro Wizard gets talked about like the ultimate “glue” card: you drop it and it just fixes problems—stuns, resets, cleans up small stuff, and bails you out of pushes. A lot of players treat it as a classic, never-really-bad pick that always has some value, even if it’s not the flashiest win con. The vibe is mostly positive and kind of underrated: not broken, just consistently useful and annoying enough that you can’t ignore it.

items.name: Elite Barbarians
items.description: A pair of upgraded Barbarians for 6 Elixir, notorious for their sheer combined speed and melee power. Together, they can wreck tanks, evaporate glass cannons and shred down building-targeting units, though they're easily distracted and are especially weak against large swarms. Brain? How about some brawn instead!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Elite Barbarians get talked about like the classic “bridge spam punish” card: if you overspend or look away for two seconds, they’ll just take your tower and make you feel stupid. A lot of players also see them as washed compared to their old terror days—still capable of stealing games, but usually not that good unless the opponent misplays. The vibe is basically mid-ladder PTSD: not respected, but always a little scary when they show up at the bridge.

items.name: Hunter
items.description: For 4 Elixir, this mustachioed man with a double-barreled gun has moderate health and can shoot a wave of bullets. This shotgun blast has a unique degree of versatility. Up close, he busts tanks. From afar, shred swarms - ground and air! Be careful, though - if he's too far away from a tank or too close to a swarm, he's a lost cause.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Hunter gets talked about like a lowkey cracked shotgun DPS card: when you place it right, it deletes tanks and chunks air for cheap and feels way stronger than its popularity suggests. The vibe is that it’s a legit, versatile defender (and can fit lots of decks), but it’s not some auto-win card—if you misplace it or can’t protect it, it just doesn’t look special. One side treats it as a really strong, reliable damage piece; the other sees it as mostly a niche pick (often tied to RG) that’s solid but not a must-run.

items.name: Executioner
items.description: For 5 Elixir, he provides insane value with moderately high hitpoints and a sharp piercing axe that damages everything, ground or air, in its path for 4 tiles. On top of that, his axe returns, dealing another swing of damage on the way back to him, dealing much more! He can obliterate tightly packed units and deal decent damage, but can still be surrounded due to his slow attack rate. Speaking about coming back, his foes can't say the same.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Executioner’s vibe is basically: defensive god when he’s paired with Tornado, but kind of mid and inconsistent when he’s not. People complain that he underperforms for the cost because he gets distracted by tanks and sometimes just doesn’t hit what you need him to hit, which makes you overcommit and get punished. At the same time, the Exe-Nado combo is seen as one of those nasty, matchup-warping defenses that feels like a cheat code when it’s set up right.

items.name: Bandit
items.description: Right Hand Man (Woman) of the Forest Gang that dashes from one unit to the other and quickly annihilates them, the Bandit is a formidable foe for 3 Elixir. When she dashes, nothing can touch her, making her great for quick & damaging attacks. However, her moderate hitpoints leave her vulnerable to swarms or high damage units.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Bandit gets talked about like the definition of “fine”: cheap bridge-spam pressure that can force a response, maybe sneak some damage, but she’s squishy and doesn’t really mini-tank or carry defenses the way you want. A lot of players feel she’s kinda fallen into the shadow of other bridge-spam options (especially the flashier replacements), so she ends up being a boring mid pick unless your deck specifically wants that dash pressure. She can still be annoying in the right moments, but most of the time people just see her as replaceable.

items.name: Royal Recruits
items.description: Six fairly tough melee ground troops spread out in a horizontal formation across the arena. With the addition of a shield to absorb otherwise devastating strikes, they can distract and repel most enemies and act as a capable shield for other units across both lanes, though this is for a hefty 7 Elixir, so use them wisely, for they come weak to huge splash damage.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Royal Recruits get talked about like this weird “why is everyone suddenly running it?” card—especially in that mid/upper ladder pocket where you just keep queuing into split-lane Recruits stuff. The vibe is that they’re not some unstoppable threat without the EVO, and in the current meta they can feel like cannon fodder that’s pretty easy to wipe if you’ve got the right answers. Still, they’re solid for split pressure and spell-baiting, so when you’re missing the clean counters they can quietly snowball into an annoying, grindy game.

items.name: Night Witch
items.description: The mean sister of the Witch, she has moderate hitpoints, moderately-high melee single-target damage and the ability to spawn supporting flying Bats for only 4 Elixir, including one after death! She can quickly swarm units and towers with her Bats, but is defenseless against splash damage.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Night Witch gets talked about like a relic from the old beatdown days—people remember the menace, but now she’s mostly just "why am I even running this?" because she’s too easy to delete and modern beatdown would rather slot whatever broken support is meta. One side basically treats her as outdated and replaceable (Golem doesn’t even want her anymore), while the other still calls her “good” on paper since she’s a 4-elixir bat-spawning support that hits air/ground. Overall vibe: not unplayable, but kind of washed unless the matchup/deck is specifically built to make her matter.

items.name: Bats
items.description: Five single-target melee units that offer good defensive and offensive value despite their very weak stats. Fragile but fast flying units, Bats can attack both ground and air units and their strong bites can easily deal with solo mini-tanks. At just 2 Elixir, Bats can fortify and cheapen your deck.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Bats get talked about like the definition of fragile: they pop to basically anything, so a lot of players just ask “why not run Minions?” and move on. The vibe is that they’re rarely worth the slot because they die instantly and don’t reliably swing games, even if they can technically get value as a cheap distraction/DPS swarm. Overall perception is more “outclassed and forgettable” than genuinely scary or toxic.

items.name: Royal Ghost
items.description: A vengeful spirit is haunting the Arena! For 3 ;Elixir, he has moderate hitpoints & area melee damage and can become invisible, being fully unable to be targeted by troops or buildings. He strikes enemies and towers before they even see him and clears swarms with ease. However, he has a slow attack speed, leaving him susceptible to other mini-tanks. All is not lost, however.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Royal Ghost gets talked about like the perfect annoying hybrid: enough HP to be a mini tank, enough DPS to punish you, and the invis pressure means you can’t just ignore it or you’re taking damage. One side of the community rates it really highly as a consistent, always-useful pressure piece that forces responses and fits tons of decks. The other side is way more lukewarm, basically treating it as just “fine” and not a standout, so the vibe is split between underrated staple and forgettable average card.

items.name: Ram Rider
items.description: She lands into the arena on her trusty ram, dashing to the nearest building while the rider snares and damages any nearby troops, slowing their travelling speed significantly. The ram deals massive damage when it collides with the tower. Like her brother, the Hog Rider, the ram is able to jump over the river, all for 5 Elixir. Nothing will stop this inseparable duo!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Ram Rider gets talked about like a polarizing card that swings between “please nerf this” and “why am I even running this,” and right now the vibe is way closer to irrelevant. It’s still a bridge-spam win con with the snare/control utility, but it’s not feeling consistent in the meta, so a lot of players treat it like a matchup-check more than a reliable ladder staple. Overall perception: not unplayable, just kind of dead unless the meta shifts back in its favor.

items.name: Zappies
items.description: If it moves, then it won’t for much longer. This 4 Elixir trio of menaces will stun a target until it is destroyed, making them exceptionally effective on defense or as support. However, be sure to protect them against swarms and splash damage due to their slow hit speed and moderately-low hitpoints. They also won't stun for forever.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Zappies are one of those cards that feel insanely obnoxious when they’re good—stun-locking, splitting lanes, and forcing awkward responses while acting as solid fireball bait. A chunk of players see them as genuinely strong and kind of underrated right now, especially when you’re already busy defending and they stack up. But there’s also a loud camp that thinks they’re basically trash outside of very specific recruits/flying-machine style shells, so the card swings hard between “secretly cracked” and “why are you running this?” depending on who you ask.

items.name: Rascals
items.description: A 5 Elixir value pack of a trio of trouble, with one boy and two girls. The boy absorbs damage from the front as mini-tank and melee attacker, while the high damage but low health girls fire from behind! With big health and big damage combined, this card is a complete wombo combo of a push by theirselves, but it's less wombo if one of their gum-slinging or sword-swinging engine(s) get(s) chopped off.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Rascals are super polarizing: one side sees them as a buffed-up bait staple that’s actually dominating right now, because even if you log the girls you’re still stuck dealing with a beefy boy that hits way harder than he used to. The other side thinks the card is straight up trash—"crappy knight plus crappy archers"—and basically free value for any solid splash/control defense. Net vibe is: either a legit log-bait powerhouse or a clown card, depending on who you ask and what you’re playing into.

items.name: Cannon Cart
items.description: This upgraded Cannon now moves and has more than double the hp! It's slick and quick too, being able to sneakily lock onto a Princess Tower in the blink of an eye and deal surprising amounts of damage if not reacted quickly. Once it reaches 50%, it activates its building form, which acts the same as a Cannon (though with different stats). The Cartless Cannon stays intact for half a minute to deter any incoming attacks. However, it's weak to swarms, air troops, and has a heftier 5 Elixir cost.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Cannon Cart gets talked about like a sneaky, high-DPS menace: if it ever locks and you don’t have the clean answer, you feel instantly punished. Some players see it as a genuinely strong, underrated threat that also doubles as a defensive building after it breaks, which makes it feel super efficient. Others basically shrug and call it underwhelming/situational, like it’s not bad but it’s not worth a slot in most decks unless you’re building around it.

items.name: Mega Knight
items.description: Ladies and gentleman, prepare to get slammed by a thousand mustaches worth of power: Mega Knight! This super soldier will land with a BOOM, dealing massive area damage to all ground opponents around him. Then he will jump to the next target in range, landing with another powerful BOOM! And if he's not jumping, he'll do a powerful smack, dealing splash damage to melee units! He has very high hitpoints for 7 Elixir, but can still be defeated by high damage units due to his slow attack.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mega Knight gets talked about like the ultimate “looks scary, isn’t actually that good” card: expensive, super distractable, and it mostly shines when people are overleveled or you’re running into players without clean answers. It can still farm value in the right spots (drop + splash control), but a lot of the time it just turns into a clunky 7-elixir commitment that hands the opponent a counterpush. Overall vibe is mid-ladder menace energy more than a reliable top-tier threat, especially without leaning on the evo.

items.name: Skeleton Barrel
items.description: A 3 Elixir barrel packed with bones that drifts through the air looking for towers and trouble. It's a bit on the fragile side, but it soars quickly through the arena. When it makes contact with a building, or when the balloons get popped, it explodes and drops seven sword-swinging Skeletons ready to party!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Skeleton Barrel gets talked about like a niche, kinda cheesy bait win-con that mostly exists to force a response or stack with other bait pressure. One side of the community view is that it’s only really scary because of its EVO; without that, it’s just “mid” and not a real threat by itself. The other vibe is basically “who cares, it just gets zapped/logged,” so it ends up feeling matchup- and spell-availability dependent more than anything.

items.name: Flying Machine
items.description: A 4 Elixir flying card with moderately-low hitpoints. Its long range and moderate damage allow it to attack from afar, often out-ranging its target, making it a great support card. Although it is quite frail, it can easily pressure the opponent and force a bad trade due to being decently afar from foes!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Flying Machine gets talked about like a washed-up "used to be annoying" ranged flier that doesn’t keep up anymore—too squishy, not enough DPS, and it just doesn’t bring the oomph into beefy metas. A lot of the time it’s framed as falling off hard post-evos/heroes, where you need real damage and it doesn’t deliver. There’s still a grudging respect that it can get value in the right scenario, but the vibe is mostly that it’s niche and kinda dead unless your matchup/deck specifically makes it work.

items.name: Wall Breakers
items.description: Those guys have broke the Arena's walls and inserted their way into the King's deck! After realizing walls don't exist gameplay-wise in Clash Royale, the daring duo runs straight for the nearest building and causes damage with their barrels of gunpowder, which explode on impact with a tower for 2 Elixir! Reacting just a second too slow often means giving the user both the damage advantage and Elixir advantage, though spells can make quick work of them.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Wall Breakers get talked about like pure spam/brain-rot pressure: you just keep tossing them at the bridge and force constant awkward responses for cheap. One side says they’ve fallen off a bit in the current meta compared to their peak, but they’re still labeled insanely annoying and oppressive when paired with the usual bait shell. Overall vibe is “low effort, high payoff” — if you’re facing them, it feels like you’re getting chipped to death while they cycle back again and again.

items.name: Royal Hogs
items.description: For 5 Elixir, four moderate health, low damage pigs that rush the enemy tower are conjured, jumping over rivers if they have to. Be warned, their speed and numbers make them difficult to stop, although area damaging units or spells can take them out easily. Despite everything, one question still get left unanswered... who let the hogs out? The question may be answered when hogs fly… wait, what? They do?
items.llm_vibe_summary: Royal Hogs get talked about like a straight-up elite win condition: split them, pressure both lanes, and suddenly your opponent’s spell cycle is getting dragged around the map. They’re loved for how efficient and hard-to-stop they feel without the perfect counters, and they’re seen as a legit meta pick rather than some niche gimmick. One side gives them extra credit for spell-bait + split-lane flexibility (even without EVO), while the other basically just stamps them as S-tier disgusting and moves on.

items.name: Goblin Giant
items.description: Not all Goblins are weak! Costing 6 Elixir, this giant packs decently good hitpoints and a decent punch as he walks straight to the Crown Tower. And he doesn't come alone: two Spear Goblins are on his back as an untouchable support, and after his demise, his spear-throwing friends will keep fighting!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Giant gets talked about like a clunky, kinda overcosted tank that dies too fast for 6, and the spear gobs on his back feel like “wet paper towel shooters” unless the deck is built perfectly around him. People still admit he can be nasty in specific setups (the classic Goblin Giant + Sparky style), but it’s very “either it pops off or it does nothing,” so it lands in that gimmicky/matchup-dependent zone. Overall vibe: not a staple meta win con, but when it’s in the right toxic-feeling combo it can be super annoying to deal with.

items.name: Fisherman
items.description: Get over here! The Fisherman pulls troops towards him with his hook so he can slow them and kill them with a slap from his daily catch. Against buildings, he pulls himself towards them to save the time and energy of walking towards them. He's a bit weak to tanks and swarms, but all this hook, line, and fish slap for only 3 Elixir? Those statistics get me hooked!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Fisherman gets talked about like the troop-version of Tornado: stupid utility, constant king activations, and it just yoinks win cons/tanks wherever it wants. People find it annoying because one hook can completely flip an interaction, but it’s also the kind of card that feels insanely satisfying when you actually know the timings and placements. Overall vibe is that it’s way more valuable than its hype level—quietly cracked and shows up a lot in serious decks.

items.name: Magic Archer
items.description: An inspiring idol for Archers (besides their Queen)! For 4 Elixir, he has moderate health, damage and the ability to send his arrow even further than his targeting range. He can safely pierce buildings from afar, as well as multiple targets within the arrow's linear path, and can easily clear swarms. However, tougher troops and heavy spells will easily destroy him.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Magic Archer gets talked about like a sweaty, lineup-reliant card: if you’re good with angles you can make it look completely unfair, but if you’re not, it’s just a squishier ranged troop that can feel inconsistent. People still call it a pain to deal with because the chip and piercing pressure forces responses and can randomly swing games off one clean geometry shot. Overall the vibe is “high-skill and annoying,” with some seeing it as newly legit after buffs, while others still prefer more consistent ranged options.

items.name: Electro Dragon
items.description: How shocking! This dragon attacks slowly and does low damage per hit for 5 Elixir, but there's more. His attacks will chain on to two secondary targets, gaining value with each unit hit. As if that wasn't enough, he stuns on hit, making him a powerful disruptor. However, alone, he's weak to tanks, glass cannons, and large swarms alike, and when he approaches a Princess Tower, a clever opponent may sacrifice a troop just to get that neat King's Tower activation.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Electro Dragon without the EVO gets talked about like it’s basically a dead slot: too little impact for the cost, inconsistent, and you’d almost always rather run a different splash/air support. The vibe flips hard when EVO is involved—then it’s the kind of chain-stun value that feels disgusting to play into, especially in those healer + E-Golem style piles. So it’s either “why would you ever play this?” or “this is making me want to uninstall,” depending on whether the EVO is in the conversation.

items.name: Firecracker
items.description: This former Archer has turned rebellious, bringing a firework to cause havoc in the arena, just to lit up the showdowns! While frail for 3 Elixir, she'll push herself back with every attack, making it harder for enemy troops to reach her. Whenever her rocket hits a target, it will split into smaller sparks that deal damage to troops or buildings behind it. But be careful; a smart opponent can use her shrapnel to activate their King's Tower!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Firecracker is the definition of “why is this getting value again?”—she sits behind a tank/win con and casually bleeds tower HP while being a constant headache to line up and remove. People complain that the base version already overperforms in the right spots, and the EVO is basically what makes her feel actually worth building around. Overall she’s talked about like a toxic, match-tilting nuisance: not always the most consistent standalone card, but when she starts chaining splash and chip, it feels disgusting to play into.

items.name: Mighty Miner
items.description: You don't need a shovel to mine skillfully! One big, strong, drilling, punch-packing hunk is spawned for 4 Elixir! He packs high health, weak damage that ramps up over time to significant measures, similar to the Inferno Dragon. He can easily melt large, tanky troops (or your tower) with just a drill, but struggles with swarms! But wait, there's more!For 1 Elixir, you can use hisExplosive Escape, which makes him drill underground and cross over to his other side, parallel to the tile he was in! Now here comes the "Explosive" part of it: when burying himself, he will leave a big bomb that explodes after one second, dealing moderate damage to troops, annihilating swarms, and pushing back units vulnerable to knockback! So when you face this mega mine muncher, prepare for climax, and expect the unexpected! His ability has a 13 second cooldown.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mighty Miner gets talked about like a solid, technical champ that can be nasty if you actually know what you’re doing—timing the ability and using the lane swap to bail yourself out or set up value. The vibe isn’t “broken” though; it’s more like he’s just kinda okay and held back by the fact you can only run one champ and there are usually better options. Some people respect it as a higher-skill pick, but it doesn’t have that must-play presence, so it ends up feeling quietly decent rather than scary.

items.name: Elixir Golem
items.description: A hefty mound of Elixir, dividing into two Elixir Golemites with half his stats that, in turn, divide into two sentient Elixir Blobs with half of their stats each, the Elixir Golem provides formidable tanking and damaging ability for the very low price of 3 Elixir. Do be warned - when your opponent manages to divide him into his lesser stages, they get 1 Elixir from the Golem and 0.5 Elixir per Golemite/Blob destroyed -  totalling 4 Elixir! This kind gift is enough to turn the tides against you, so make sure this 4 Elixir is spent the worst way possible!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Elixir Golem gets talked about like straight-up coin-flip, brain-rot gameplay: you drop it, stack healer/drag type stuff behind it, and either your opponent’s deck can handle it or you just steamroll. People don’t just dislike it—they treat it like a toxic archetype that ruins the vibe, especially when it’s paired with the usual support package that makes everything feel unkillable. Even when it’s not top-tier consistent, it still gets labeled no-skill because the “outplay” factor feels low and the matchup lottery feels high.

items.name: Battle Healer
items.description: Selflessly, the Battle Healer has relatively high hitpoints and medium damage for her 4 Elixir cost. Whenever she is deployed or hits something, she heals herself and all troops around herself. Even though she has wings, she cannot attack air units. Although her damage and healing effect may initially seem weak, her healing will eventually snowball enough so that allied troops survive opposing pushes, creating a deadly pressure strategy.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Battle Healer gets talked about like an objectively trash card… until it’s in that E-Golem/E-Drag pile where it suddenly turns into a two-minute, can’t-die nightmare. People hate how it punishes even tiny overcommits—if you slip once, it just perma-heals and the whole lane becomes unkillable. The vibe is basically “cheesy coin-flip”: outside the toxic combo decks it’s whatever, but in the right shell it feels disgusting to play against.

items.name: Skeleton King
items.description: For 4 Elixir, the almighty dynast of the dead rises through the Arena, clubbing everything through his way with a moderate damage melee splash attack! While he stays in the Arena, any form of life destroyed in the entire battlefield, whether it be friend or foe, will make a fine addition to his Soul Counter, albeit with a limit of 14 souls.For 2 Elixir, you can use hisSoul Summoningability right above your hand. It will release all of the souls he kept for battle in form of Skeletons around a royal Graveyard aura around him, and adds in 6 more Skeletons among the skeleton squad! Be a bit cautious, as some splash damage dealers will shrug off his reincarnated henchmen and kill a royal entity not even he could revive. It also has a 20 second cooldown, and reset the Skeleton King's soul counter.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Skeleton King gets talked about like a bait/graveyard glue card that can randomly turn a push into a problem if you stack souls and pop ability at the right time. At the same time, a chunk of players straight up think he’s trash for a champ slot—too often you don’t get the big moment and he just feels like a worse option than other mini-tank pressure pieces. So you’ll see people call him sneaky good in the right shell, and others call him overrated and not worth building around.

items.name: Archer Queen
items.description: The queen and coach of all Archers. A powerful glass cannon rocks the Arena with her crossbow and its mean bolts that she constantly fires slowly for 5 Elixir.For 1 Elixir, you can use herCloaking Capeability, mitigating the glass part of her by turning herself invisible, just like the Royal Ghost: unable to be targeted for 3.5 seconds! For the cherry on top, she also increases her fire output for herself drastically while under her invisibility cape, dishing out more impressive loads of damage onto her foes while still being safe inside her hidden cloak! She's vulnerable to heavy spells and large swarms, and walks slower under her maybe heavy cape, but with great power comes great responsibility and a 17 second cooldown, so be careful!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Archer Queen is seen as a super versatile, high-value champion that you can slap into a lot of decks and just get paid off, mainly because the ability can swing fights and farm value. The vibe around her is pretty tryhard/top-ladder-ish: if someone’s running Queen, they’re usually playing something real and they know what they’re doing. Compared to more niche champions, she’s talked about as the safer, more universally useful pick rather than some gimmick.

items.name: Golden Knight
items.description: The Golden Knight makes a force through the Arena, slashing enemies through the power of his moderate damaging sword and absorbing through his very healthy golden bodyplate, all for 4 Elixir.For 1 Elixir, you can use his dashinglyDashing Dash, causing him to run around until he approaches an enemy, instantly dashing through a foe through another for up to 10 times, and delivering mean swings afterwards! On top of everything else, very much like his former associate, the Bandit, he is completely invulnerable to anything while dashing! Combine that with his constant dashing without a skip, and you get a juggernaut who's figuratively and literally gold. The ability has a 8 second cooldown, and once he dashes onto a tower, he stops dashing, no matter how many units he has hit before.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Golden Knight gets talked about like the “actually good” champion: a legit mini-tank that can randomly swing the whole game when the dash chains right. People respect it because you can’t just slam it and pray—you need timing and spacing to not waste the ability, and good players make it look disgusting. Even after nerfs, the vibe is still that it’s top-tier pressure and one of the scariest punish tools when you’re even a little off-cycle.

items.name: Monk
items.description: Make the opponents wish they've never fought in the first place with this peacemaking powerhouse! With 5 Elixir, a tank is spawned, attacking through foes through melee attacks, with the third one dealing triple the damage of said attack AND pushing the victim a few tiles back.For 1 Elixir, you can use hisPensive Protection, an ability that makes him meditate for a while and chill out. But not only that, a zen shield forms around himself, making him take less damage! When a projectile approaches it, he'll throw hands, nullifying AND deflecting it to the nearest Crown Tower. It can deflect a lot from fire to ice. And yes, it can deflect projectile SPELLS. That includes Rockets. It can deflect as long as it is travelling in the air with velocity not too high, meaning it cannot deflect stuff like Inferno Beams, stunning electricity, or a sword.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Monk gets talked about like a card that should be scary in theory, but most of the time it just kinda underperforms and you don’t really feel like you’re missing it. When it does show up, it’s usually glued to Royal Giant and that combo is what makes people tilt—one good ability timing and it can swing a whole interaction. Overall the vibe is “situational and annoying when it works,” not “meta-defining carry.”

items.name: Skeleton Dragons
items.description: These smaller, skeletal cousins of the Baby Dragon move just as fast while packing almost double the punch when together! However, their bones seem to be a bit brittle, as they have much less health, and their belches don't cover as wide of an area. Their 4 Elixir cost also hasn't changed, meaning most of their utility comes down to how long they can avoid having their bones scattered. But there are two of them, so they can help defend in both lanes simultaneously.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Skeleton Dragons get talked about like they straight-up disappeared from the game—most people just run Baby Dragon instead, so you basically never see them. When they do show up, it’s usually as a niche splash/air support pick (like into Recruits), but the vibe is that they’re outclassed and not worth the slot. Overall perception: low presence, low hype, and mostly a “who even plays this anymore?” card.

items.name: Mother Witch
items.description: For 4 Elixir, this witch cackles wildly as she curses her foes, causing them to turn into Cursed Hogs if they fall during the curse's effect. Against a large swarm of units, she can quickly create an army to assault opposing buildings! However, her damage is low, so bulky units will shrug off her curse and make quick work of her, and the curse lasts after 5 seconds. Save her for an opportune moment in order to start cooking up a pig stew!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mother Witch is basically the “plug-and-play” hate card: when swarm/bait or Graveyard is popular, she can just auto-farm value and make people feel cooked. A lot of players find her super annoying because she can flip a defense into a disgusting counterpush if you slip up. But there’s a real split in perception—some think she’s a meta counterpiece, others think she’s kinda trash/situational and only looks good when the matchup feeds her pigs.

items.name: Electro Spirit
items.description: Costing a shockingly low 1 Elixir, the Electro Spirit shows his killer mustache to the table. Although his damage and stun duration don't match that of the Ice Spirit, his attack will chain onto up to nine units within four tiles of each other, allowing for some truly shocking effects that can stretch far across the arena, even all the way to your opponent's King Tower!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Electro Spirit gets talked about like an auto-include 1-elixir glue card: cheap cycle that still flips interactions with chain stun, resets, and clutch defense. People rate it up with the best spirits because it’s never really “bad” in any matchup and it’s always getting value when you time it right. The vibe is less ‘toxic’ and more ‘clean and efficient’—a little tryhard, but in a respectable way.

items.name: Electro Giant
items.description: Don't get too close! This shocking version of the Giant has a Zap Pack, which zaps back anything nearby that attacks him, making swarms and fast attackers disappear in a matter of seconds. Although his movement speed is fairly slow, he is still capable of destroying a Princess Tower all by himself! However, he has a hefty cost of 7 Elixir, so players must wait for the perfect opportunity to electrify their opponent. Also, try to tell him his finger guns are fake and see how much voltage your body absorbs.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Electro Giant gets talked about like pure matchup roulette: you drop it and either they’ve got the building/mini-tank answers and you do nothing, or you just roll them and it feels disgusting. A lot of players label it straight-up no-skill/brain-dead because the gameplan is basically “slam E-Giant and pray,” and it’s miserable to face when you don’t have the clean counters. At the same time, there’s also the take that it’s kind of rough in the current meta and can feel borderline unusable into all the common answers—so the frustration is real, but the consistency isn’t.

items.name: Phoenix
items.description: At the hot price of 4 Elixir, this majestic flying bird fills his role as figurative and literal heat. He packs moderately low health, with decent damage, and to compensate, he will lay an Egg (topped with death damage) when he dies. The egg has low health, and if it isn't destroyed quick enough, it (unsurprisingly, but epicly) spawns a brand new smaller Phoenix. One should consider protecting the egg it lays with a mini-tank. But if they do, they'll consider small spells to extinguish out the fire it lays.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Phoenix gets talked about like it fell off hard—slow, low pressure, and usually not worth the slot compared to other air supports. The only time it feels disgusting is when the egg actually gets value and you don’t have the answer, which makes it randomly irritating in certain matchups. Overall the vibe is “kinda dead,” with occasional annoying moments when the respawn swings an interaction.

items.name: Little Prince
items.description: Now who was the irresponsible parent that let their nephew to the Arena? This mischievous brat loads in his crossbow and fires them at all enemies. His crossbow attack ramps up in DPS the longer he stays still, and unlike the Inferno Dragon, he doesn't stop his crossbow's ramping up when he destroys an enemy. He does stop when he moves an inch, though, making knockback come in handy, as well as stunners.For 3 Elixir, you can use hisRoyal Rescueto meet his guardian: Guardienne, creating the most mischievous tank + glass cannon combo. She dashes her way from behind the kid and on the way to be in front of the Little Prince himself, damaging and knocking back enemies during her dash, and hopefully letting said Little Prince stay a little longer to charge his crossbow. The Guardienne will act like a Knight as long as it stays alive. The ability has a 30 second cooldown.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Community vibe on Little Prince is basically “used to be insane, now it’s kinda cooked.” People clown on the ability being a super overpriced, rarely-worth-it button (it’s easy to point at a million better 6-elixir options), so the card ends up feeling gimmicky and underwhelming most games. There’s still some respect for players who can actually time and justify the ability, but the dominant perception is that it’s the worst-feeling champ and not really doing enough in the meta.

items.name: Goblin Demolisher
items.description: For 4 Elixir, this Goblin is packed with explosions and dynamite, throwing them from 5 tiles away! However when he's at half health, he keeps his priorities STRAIGHT, rushing through the nearest building quickly. Once he falls to his demise, he goes out with a BANG; literally! His death explosion gives out high damage and pushback to every enemy in its large radius.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Community vibe on Goblin Demolisher is that it’s a super annoying little utility piece—mostly seen in drill shells—because it can chip, splash, and randomly turn into a wall-breaker style threat for just 4 elixir. It’s not treated like some game-breaking win con, but more like a consistently solid, “why is this getting value again?” support card. Overall perception leans underrated: people who face it feel the pressure and chip adds up, while a lot of players still don’t rate it until it steals damage or bails out a defense with knockback.

items.name: Goblin Machine
items.description: We don't know if something so puny as a Goblin built this mech, but we do know that this machine is anything but puny! For 5 Elixir, a massive mech made of wood and metal is deployed into the arena, having high health and low damage with every punch. Though, the true star of the show is the Rocket, dealing high damage to both ground and air units after a long 3.5 second charge time, but only a third of its damage is dealt to Crown Towers. Additionally, this Rocket has a dead zone to where any troop or building less than 2.5 tiles in front of the machine isn’t targeted, helping the machine deal with those pesky ranged troops much easier.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Community vibe is that Goblin Machine was only “fun” when it was completely busted—aka you’d just get deleted by it—and once it got nerfed, it basically stopped existing. Now it gets talked about like a failed concept: either it’s broken and miserable, or it’s balanced by being trash. Compared to other 5-elixir support options, people feel like it doesn’t justify the slot anymore.

items.name: Suspicious Bush
items.description: For 2 Elixir, this totally normal bush crawls towards the nearest building, being completely invisible to every troop and building that comes by it. However, once it reaches the nearest building or is destroyed by a spell, 2 Bush Goblins with low hitpoints and moderate damage jump out of the absolutely real and stationary bush and attack whatever they see! Although it instantly folds to spells and splash damage since its true health is super low, it's still a deceptive tacti- I mean, a completely ordinary bush!!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Suspicious Bush gets talked about like a dumb cheap pressure card that forces a response for only 2 elixir, and if you blink it can randomly chunk your tower. It fits that hyper-bait, spammy playstyle where you just keep tossing it in and your opponent is stuck spending answers over and over. The vibe is mostly “annoying but effective,” and it’s seen as sneakily strong for how little it costs.

items.name: Goblinstein
items.description: IT'S ALI-I-I-I-VE! Doctor Goblinstein is a mad scientist ready to drop in the arena with his mad inventions, such as his trusty Monster, delicately crafted with high health and a vendetta for buildings, although Goblinstein forgot that his creation's a bit vulnerable to tank killers when the Doctor's not in. He holds a remote that shoots some lightning, stunning enemies for a split second, while being safely protected by the green hunky creation of a mastermind. These 2 can attack and/or defend one or two lanes, all for only 5 Elixir!For 2 Elixir, you can use hisLightning Link, which is quite shocking for him and the Monster! Goblinstein will link through the monster's antenna, creating an electrical passage between them, dealing a shocking amount of damage to anything brave enough to stand near the electricity for a while. If his Monster dies, he'll drop his antenna, which will still go on! The ability has a 17 second cooldown, and it immediately stops once the Doctor is defeated. Even if the Monster is alive, the ability cannot be activated.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblinstein went from looking like a whatever/low-impact champ to being a legit meta piece, mostly because it slots insanely well into Graveyard and makes those pushes feel way harder to stop. People see it as a tanky support/control body where the ability can just swing interactions and bail you out, plus the stun value adds up fast. The main knock is it’s kinda married to Graveyard—outside that, it’s not really a plug-and-play champ like the more universally useful picks.

items.name: Rune Giant
items.description: Don't mess with this bulky and big woman! For 4 Elixir, cheapest of the Giants, you get a powerful blacksmith! And what's better than a blacksmith? A blacksmith that enchants up to 2 of your troops' weapon of choice! They deal extra damage on every third hit, meaning that troops that hit quickly will deal extra damage quicker, while slow hitters with high damage per hit will be even more devastating! She will enchant another troop if one of her enchanted troops die. Keep her alive for as long as possible, since the enchantments run out after 5 seconds.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Rune Giant gets talked about like the actual worst card: not tanky enough to be a real tank, not impactful enough to be real support, and it ends up being a useless hybrid that doesn’t defend or pressure. Even after buffs, people act like it only “works” with a tiny handful of niche synergies, so most of the time playing it feels like you’re trolling.
