Overall vibe is just dead-on-arrival and outclassed by basically any mini tank, DPS unit, or actual win con you could slot instead.

items.name: Berserker
items.description: Her name is exactly what you're thinking! She won't hesitate to tear you apart for only 2 Elixir! She attacks very fast and is resistant to spells, though her damage is low, so she'll have to be supported accordingly in order to go berserk mode. Beware though, if her mom, Rune Giant, is around, the Berserker will be even more dangerous!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Berserker gets talked about like a lowkey cracked 2-elixir glue card: it’s that perfect stopgap between mini-tank and damage dealer that bails you out of bad spots and doesn’t feel like a useless cycle slot. The vibe is that it quietly farms value as a distraction / “get out of jail” defender, and the stats/usage backing it up makes people treat it like a sleeper meta pick rather than a flashy win con.

items.name: Boss Bandit
items.description: The boss of the secret Forest Gang association, and the one on all the wanted posters. For 6 Elixir, the Boss Bandit is far more powerful than the Bandit, with far more dangerous and damaging hits and dashes, while making her harder to take out.For 1 Elixir, you can use herGetaway Grenade, she turns invisible while teleporting back 6 tiles, making her dash again if her target stays away! The ability can be used a total of 2 times, building her a great getaway (and grenade) plan!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Boss Bandit gets talked about like a straight-up headache card: if you’re down elixir or don’t have the exact answers in hand, it can just run you over and make you feel helpless. People describe it as ability-spam/"press button to win" energy—low effort, high payoff, and super frustrating to face. There’s a split where some admit it can be scary when played smart, but the dominant vibe is still that it’s toxic, cheesy, and way too easy to get value with.

items.name: Cannon
items.description: For 3 Elixir, you get a building which has moderate hitpoints, damage, and one of the shortest lifetimes out of all the buildings. Doesn't seem like much, does it? Well, when you see how much this building has potential for such a low cost, you'll reconsider and use it nearly every time. After 2 Cycles, if itsEvolutionis active, the Cannon learns a new skill from the Eagle Artillery:Barrage Carnage! When deployed, he Cannon shoots 9 barrage shots (5 in the most distant position and 4 in the closest position). Half of the shots hit the other side, and all of the shots knock back, making it great for offense and defense.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Cannon gets talked about like the cheap, no-BS defensive building that just deletes pushes for 3 elixir—tons of DPS, easy kites, and it bails you out when you’re getting swarmed. People frame it as a staple in cycle/control because it’s hard to justify more expensive buildings when Cannon does the job and lets you keep tempo. The vibe is mostly “quietly cracked/underrated” rather than flashy—when it’s placed right, it feels stupid efficient.

items.name: Goblin Hut
items.description: Here come the Spear Goblins! For 4 Elixir, the building will take a nap when there is no action, but once a unit gets at least 6 tiles close to this tribal-looking building, a Spear Goblin will come out of it every 2.1 seconds! This can happen for as long as there is a troop in the Goblin Hut's activation range. And just when you think they'll stop coming, another Spear Goblin pops out upon the hut's death! They can be amazing on defense, while easily building up behind a tank to cause even more damage on a counterpush, but are still weak to splash damage. The Goblin Hut lasts 30 seconds, so make half a minute count!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Hut gets talked about like the kind of card that just won’t let you play the game—constant spear goblin drip-feed, nonstop distractions, and it always forces a spell or you start bleeding value. A lot of players see it as low-effort, spammy control that snowballs just by existing, but others rate it highly because it’s tanky, consistent, and makes the rest of your deck feel better by baiting responses all game. Overall vibe: irritating pressure generator that can feel toxic when it’s meta.

items.name: Mortar
items.description: It's quite hard to stop these explosive shells from hitting a tower and dealing quite a bit of damage thanks to its blind spot; any enemy troop that gets too close will be ignored. The ability to snipe towers, its moderate hitpoints, and the light cost of 4 Elixir make this building versatile, though its incredibly slow hit speed means it often needs additional support against any unit that isn't one shot by its attack.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mortar’s got that “I respect it, but it kinda sucks” energy: people see it as a sweaty, high-skill win con that you’re grinding all game just to get one lock. A big chunk of the community vibe is that base Mortar is basically a defensive building / cycle button and the card only really feels legit because of EVO Mortar propping the archetype up. Still, Mortar players get some respect because when it does connect, it chunks towers and it’s not the kind of thing you can just autopilot.

items.name: Inferno Tower
items.description: Things get really hot with this building. For 5 Elixir, this incinerator will roast even massive tanks and powerful support units in an instant for a neutral, if not positive, Elixir trade. However, beware of swarms and the power of electrickery, as this building will be overwhelmed and paralyzed.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Inferno Tower gets talked about like a solid, classic tank-melter that can hard-stop certain pushes, but it’s not something people are hyping as meta-defining right now. The vibe is mostly that it swings matchups hard—into some decks it feels amazing, and into others it’s just kind of whatever—so it lands more in that “fine, but not crazy” bucket rather than being toxic or brain-dead.

items.name: Bomb Tower
items.description: Bombs away! This ground-only building throws bombs dealing moderate splash damage at a decent rate, making it perfect against swarms. Combined with its medium hitpoints, its cost of 4 Elixir, and a death bomb, this building is one of the most effective against ground units in the game. It attacks quite slowly for its damage though, so tankier units will only take a few scratches from the explosives.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Bomb Tower gets talked about like a niche defensive building that only really shows up in a couple specific shells, and outside of that it feels forgettable. One side basically calls it weak for the cost and says it “hits like a wet paper towel,” while the other thinks it’s at least improved and not the worst—just not something you’re excited to run. Overall the vibe is: it can stabilize ground pushes, but it’s not a scary meta staple and often you’d rather run a cheaper building plus extra DPS.

items.name: Barbarian Hut
items.description: This crude but high-hitpoint barrack spawns a trio of Barbarians every 15 seconds, who deal quite a bit of damage when ignored. These Barbarians can be valuable as support, granting a significant Elixir advantage. Its death results in an additional Barbarian spawned for extra destruction. However, this hut is expensive, costing a whole 6 Elixir, and the Barbarians can be easily taken out if handled correctly.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Barbarian Hut gets talked about like the definition of a dead card: it’s not “secretly underrated,” it’s just ass and basically only shows up when someone’s trolling. When it does get value, it’s usually because the opponent mindlessly feeds tanks into the spawned barbs, not because the card is actually good. Overall vibe is gimmick building that doesn’t justify the elixir in real matches.

items.name: Tesla
items.description: Peek-a-boo! For 4 Elixir, it has moderate health and damage, pulling everything to itself, so that it can zap enemies, although it won't stun like most electric-based cards do. When nobody is near it, the Tesla hides underground, preventing any and all damage sources, except for an Earthquake. Freeze will also affect it, making it stuck on either the hidden or the revealed state.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Tesla’s perception is split hard: one side sees base Tesla as kind of a washed, low-oomph building that’s easy to spell down, distract, or misplace, so it ends up feeling like a “why would I run this without the EVO?” pick. The other side treats Tesla as a staple defensive building that’s just straight-up nasty and always solid, especially in tighter, more tryhard control/cycle contexts. Overall vibe is: either a dead-ish base card carried by upgrades, or a timeless defense tool depending on who you ask.

items.name: Elixir Collector
items.description: You gotta spend Elixir to make Elixir! For 6 Elixir, this passive building has moderate health along with the ability to produce bonus Elixir, giving you a total of 8 Elixir while alive. This will make large pushes much easier to play, although this building comes with significant risk due to its vulnerability to spells and high cost. Elixir Collector will not appear in your starting cards.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Elixir Collector feels like pure matchup roulette: if they’ve got Lightning/Rocket, you look stupid; if they don’t, you’re suddenly drowning in elixir and the game turns into a snowball. People mostly talk about it as an annoying “you HAVE to spell this” card that forces awkward responses and then punishes you for spending that spell. It’s not really hype or flashy—just a tryhard, slot-machine enabler that makes certain decks feel miserable to play into.

items.name: X-Bow
items.description: This 6 Elixir automatic crossbow has moderate hitpoints, low damage, a long range and a very fast hit speed. It can quickly whittle down small swarms and moderate-health troops, cripple tanks, and destroy towers. However, it can be very easily distracted, and is easily destroyed by spells and heftier swarms.
items.llm_vibe_summary: X-Bow gets treated like the sweaty, nerdy win con that’s actually legit if you can play it—most people respect it because you’re basically fighting the whole match just to get one lock. It’s seen as more of a top-ladder/“specialist” card than something you’d recommend to newer players, but in the right hands it’s nasty, especially with defensive setups. Compared to stuff like Mortar, it’s framed as the stronger, more reliable version of that siege grind.

items.name: Tombstone
items.description: A cheap spawner with moderate hitpoints at the cost of only 3 Elixir that spawns two Skeletons every 3.5 seconds. When destroyed, 4 more spooky scary Skeletons pop up to take down the destroyer. The spawned Skeletons and the death spawn ones can easily distract and take out single-target units, making this building very versatile.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Tombstone is one of those cards people either forget exists or swear by as a super clean, low-commitment defensive building—great for stalling/kiting tanks and making stuff like Prince, Giant, and Pekka feel miserable. Some players talk like it barely has a “real deck” anymore and just isn’t that relevant, while others see it as crazy versatile and always getting value when you need to slow the game down. Overall vibe: quietly solid defense tech, but not something everyone agrees is meta-defining.

items.name: Furnace
items.description: This 4 Elixir metallic oven has magically grown feet and arms, making him being able to slowly move and attack at long range. But the oven is not for some Show-And-Tell presentation, as he will spawn Fire Spirits out of it every 7 seconds, efficiently countering swarms and chipping down other troops while putting the opponent under pressure offensively. However, beware of spells, splash damage, and tanks, as they will put out the ongoing fire!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Furnace is super polarizing: when it’s good, it’s that annoying chip-and-splash building that forces a response every time and makes the rest of the deck feel way more oppressive. One side of the community treats it like a genuinely strong, high-range control piece that keeps generating value even without the evo, while the other side basically calls it forgettable trash and one of those cards that shouldn’t even exist in decks anymore. Overall vibe is “ugh, I have to spell this” mixed with “why am I even running this card?” depending on who you ask.

items.name: Goblin Cage
items.description: For 4 Elixir, you can drop a building containing a Goblin Brawler on the arena, pulling Hogs, Giants, and Golems alike. That cage won't hold for long though, as after a little while if it's not broken, the Goblin Brawler inside does the job himself! When released, he gets his revenge for being captured on every enemy in his sight, dishing out impressive damage while having moderate health!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Cage gets talked about like a “fine, but why?” defensive building: it can work, but a lot of the time the brawler pops out and just gets deleted by whatever support troop is behind the tank, so it doesn’t feel like it actually stabilizes the push. Some players still call it solid (even mention it getting buffed), but the overall vibe is that it’s rarely the building you *want*—more like a decent option when it lines up, and forgettable when it doesn’t.

items.name: Goblin Drill
items.description: These Goblins have abandoned locomotion with wooden barrels; now they've got a whole machine! For 4 Elixir, this drill can pop up anywhere in the arena and start releasing Goblins. Upon destruction, it spawns 2 more! At the end of the day, a bunch of Goblins are still Goblins, so area damage units or spells will do well to quell them. But be mindful of when the opponent can send it in, or your Crown Tower might be stolen too!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Drill gets talked about like a real deck card, but not some auto-win menace anymore—if people have solid Drill counters, you’re fighting for scraps and it can feel rough. The vibe is that it still wins games in the right hands (and you’ll see it up high), but it’s not the kind of win condition you just slam into anything and expect to farm. Some players frame it as a legit, skill-y control/bait win con, while others basically warn it’s a frustrating grind unless the matchup lines up.

items.name: Fireball
items.description: It's a bird! It's a plane! It's a… meteor? No! A Fireball! A large fiery rock pummels the Arena and anything else in its small radius, dealing significant damage to all enemy troops and buildings, air or ground, all for 4 Elixir. It can even knock back some ground troops! It easily destroys low hitpoint troops and cripples medium hitpoint units.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Fireball is basically the “default good spell” that just always gets value—clean damage, deletes a ton of mid-cost troops, and slots into almost any deck without feeling weird. People talk about it like a staple/tryhard tool: not flashy, just nasty and reliable, especially when paired with a small spell to finish key units. Compared to heavier spells like Rocket, it’s seen as way more consistently viable and easier to justify in most archetypes.

items.name: Arrows
items.description: Shoot into the sky with a 3 Elixir rain of Arrows. Its moderately high damage and large radius makes clearing swarms and weak enemies a breeze, but is lackluster against higher health units like the Knight or Giant.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Arrows gets talked about like the ultimate “can’t go wrong” spell: consistent, easy value, and always relevant when bait is everywhere. People rate it as one of the best all-around spells because it just deletes swarms on demand and stays useful across metas. The only real knock is that sometimes you’d rather have a heavier spell for specific problems, but Arrows is still seen as a super safe, staple pick.

items.name: Rage
items.description: Pump up your troops with a 2 Elixir boost! The Rage increases the attack speed and movement speed of all units in its effective radius, greatly multiplying their strength and creating savage beasts that can take out towers in seconds. It also deals some damage for that extra support! However, when used in the wrong place or time, it can just be a waste of Elixir.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Rage is getting talked about like it’s way better than it has any right to be: it’s not just a boost anymore, it’s basically a mini-spell that also speeds your whole push up. People see it as a legit meta tool (not a meme), especially because it can wipe little stuff while making your units feel impossible to stop. It’s not the most “toxic” card by itself, but it definitely creates those annoying, snowbally moments where one good Rage turns a normal defense into a disaster.

items.name: Rocket
items.description: For a hefty 6 Elixir you can destroy a push, a building, or... your Elixir. The ridiculously high damage comes with a ridiculously small area of effect and a long delay, so be sure not to miss. Recommended targets include support troops, expensive buildings, and Crown Towers — bingo if you hit all three!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Rocket’s seen as that high-commitment “highlight” spell: it can look insane when you snipe a big clump or steal a game, but most of the time it’s awkward to just jam into decks compared to safer big spells. People talk about it like a niche, deck-specific win path (and sometimes a surprise factor), not a universally solid option. Overall vibe is more gimmick/control-tryhard than straight-up broken—when it works it feels genius, when it doesn’t you just wish it was Fireball/Poison/Lightning.

items.name: Goblin Barrel
items.description: 3 Goblins for 3 Elixir that fly in a barrel that can be thrown anywhere in the Arena — perfect for a surprise attack! If left ignored, it can deal heavy damage to towers for an unparalleled low cost, but these Goblins are easily countered by swarms or spells and are relatively slow to begin attacking.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Barrel is basically the face of bait: cheap, spammy pressure that forces you to have the right small spell or you just start bleeding tower HP. A big chunk of the salt comes from how miserable it feels when it’s paired with the rest of the bait package—multiple threats, constant spell-bait loops, and you’re never allowed to relax. At the same time, people also point out that the *base* Barrel by itself can feel kinda awful into double-spell or solid answers, and a lot of its success comes from the deck around it (and EVO power) more than the standalone card.

items.name: Freeze
items.description: Freeze! This 4 Elixir spell incapacitates and slightly damages troops and buildings for 4 seconds. It can catch players off guard, helps your troops win combat unscathed, and can often clutch a victory, or use it as a last second defense by freezing enemy troops that is just about to reach your tower. But beware, as it buys time for your opponent to create an even bigger push by amassing new troops on the frozen ones, which will make defending even harder.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Freeze is basically the “annoying ass” spell: it doesn’t always feel like a surprise because you can usually smell it coming, but it still steals interactions and swings games when paired with a push. People acknowledge it can get value (even more after damage buffs), but it’s also easy to misplay and can straight-up hand your opponent a huge counterpush if you freeze wrong. Overall it’s viewed more like cheesy control/support than a clean, consistent top-tier spell.

items.name: Mirror
items.description: This Mirror's magic allows one to replay the last card deployed for +1 Elixir, but with one more level than the respective level of the Mirror. It can be used to double up attacks for a high reward, but results in a huge Elixir disadvantage if not used well, meaning that it's also a high risk. it can also be used to play the same card again after the original gets countered by a spell. Mirror will not appear in your starting hand.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Mirror gets talked about like the definition of “cute surprise that almost never pays off.” Most people see it as a troll/gimmick slot that gives you one cheesy win every handful of games, but usually it’s just extra elixir for the same result you could’ve gotten by running a real card. The vibe is basically: unreliable, not meta, and only worth it if you’re trying to catch someone sleeping.

items.name: Lightning
items.description: For 6 Elixir, Lightning strikes the three units with the highest hitpoints in a large area, either crippling or completely annihilating them. As a bonus, there's an extra half-second stun at the end! It's great when taking out support units in a huge push, but is utterly worthless against small units and swarms.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Lightning gets talked about like the “serious” big spell: it deletes buildings and stacked support troops and pretty reliably prints positive trades, so it just stays relevant no matter what. It’s basically always treated as an S-tier staple spell you can lean on when the meta is full of annoying backline units and defensive setups. Not really a hype or fun card—more like the sweaty, consistent option that just works.

items.name: Zap
items.description: 2 Elixir, instant effect and stunning. What's not to like in this bottle of voltage? The Zap is often the lifeline against swarms, with immediate low damage that can cripple or destroy small troops. Forces enemies to re-target, resets attacks or brings that slight extra damage to finish things off.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Zap gets talked about like the definition of a solid, honest spell: instant, consistent, and it just does its job—resetting stuff and cleaning up small units without needing some crazy setup. It’s not usually framed as “broken” or flashy, just one of those staples you can toss into tons of decks and not feel bad about. People mainly nitpick it by comparing it to other small spells (Log/Barb Barrel), but the overall vibe is still that it’s a reliable, always-playable option.

items.name: Poison
items.description: A 4 Elixir area-denial spell that maintains a powerful presence. It is capable of disrupting defenses, crippling pushes, and preventing supports being played by destroying any swarms and many glass cannons that pass through an area in a matter of seconds – but only if they stay inside it! Speaking of staying inside, in its ring of toxicity, troops walk slightly slower, aggravating the hurt put on them.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Poison gets talked about like one of those always-safe, always-useful spells: it slows, controls space, and just quietly farms value in a ton of matchups. People especially rate it because Graveyard is everywhere, so Poison both enables that style and hard-checks it at the same time. Overall it’s seen as a staple, high-utility pick rather than some cheesy or brain-dead gimmick.

items.name: Graveyard
items.description: This portable 5 Elixir ritual starts a Skeleton party anywhere in the Arena! With its timed release of an entire Skeleton Army, the Graveyard can easily overrun towers if not countered with small units or area damage, and can also act as a last-second distraction later in the game.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Graveyard gets talked about like it’s stupidly consistent right now—drop it and you’re basically guaranteed chip even when it’s “countered,” which makes it feel toxic and miserable to face. A lot of people frame it as one of the easier win cons in the meta because the deck defends forever and still squeezes damage out. At the same time, some players insist it’s not pure brain-dead since you have to scout their counters first (poison, dark goblin, etc.) or you just throw the game.

items.name: The Log
items.description: It rolls. It crushes. It knocks back. It makes those heads roll. It's none other than The Log. In a glorious entrance accompanied by a holy choir chanting "LOG!", a spiked log rolls across the Arena floor, dealing moderate damage and knocking back every enemy ground unit in its path for just 2 Elixir! Recommended targets include low health support troops, charging enemies, and every ground swarm. It can't hit air units, so don't get too comfortable! It seeks the Lumberjack with all its rage, their history go way back…
items.llm_vibe_summary: The Log is seen as one of those forever-staple spells: super consistent, easy to get value with, and it just does what it’s supposed to do every match. People talk about it like the definition of "solid"—reliable knockback, clean swarm control, and it fits basically anywhere. The only real knock is that it’s getting edged out a bit by Barb Barrel lately, since Barb Barrel does a lot of the same stuff but leaves a body behind.

items.name: Tornado
items.description: The weather forecast signals a big tornado coming after you! This windy Tornado pulls any troops in its large effective radius, quickly grouping them all up in an instant (perfect for any incoming splash damage) while also slightly damaging them for only 3 Elixir! When used correctly, this spell can be invaluable. Also useful for dragging that pesky Hog Rider to activate your King Tower.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Tornado gets talked about like pure utility: it’s the glue spell that always finds value—king activations, buying time, yanking win cons off your tower, and setting up splash synergies. People rate it as a staple that fits basically anywhere and stays relevant no matter the meta. The vibe is less “toxic” and more “annoyingly effective,” especially when it’s paired with big splash defenders (the classic pull-into-death combo).

items.name: Clone
items.description: The Clone is an incredibly volatile spell that clones every troop within its radius. For only 3 Elixir, it doubles the damage and can overwhelm opponents as Clones still retain the abilities of their normal counterparts. Despite this, each cloned troop only has 1 hitpoint, so beware of area damage! Clone can affect Champions, but only the original will use its ability. Also, it doesn't affect buildings (except the Cannon Cart before its building state) nor Skeletons summoned by the almighty Skeleton King.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Clone gets talked about like a straight-up gimmick spell: funny when it works, useless most of the time, and basically only shows up in troll/cheese decks. People see it as a “gotcha” win button that relies on the opponent not reading the obvious setup, so it feels coin-flippy rather than solid. It’s been described as not truly viable for years—if you want consistent value, you just play something else.

items.name: Earthquake
items.description: Rocking the ground for 3 Elixir, this spell is perfect for clearing small swarms, slowing units down, and doing even BIGGER damage to buildings! Even if there are no buildings in the opponent's deck, this spell can still deal decent damage to Crown Towers. However, it doesn't damage flying units (it is an EARTHquake, after all).
items.llm_vibe_summary: Earthquake gets talked about like a super niche spell: it’s only really “the thing” in Hog EQ, and outside of that it feels like the odd man out compared to Fireball/Poison/Lightning. One side basically sees it as not used much and kinda ass because most decks would rather have a real big spell. The other side treats it like an S-tier staple spell (because spells are mandatory anyway), so the vibe is split—but overall it reads as matchup-dependent and not a plug-and-play pick.

items.name: Barbarian Barrel
items.description: An angry Barbarian in a barrel rolls a moderate distance, dealing moderate damage to troops and buildings, then breaks open and goes to town with his sword. Despite falling short of reaching the Crown Towers, for 2 Elixir this card can protect, attack, and distract all at the same time.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Barbarian Barrel gets talked about like the small spell that just never stops being good: easy value, constant utility, and it straight-up outclasses Log right now because you also get a body on the board. People love how it deletes annoying ranged units while the spawned Barb immediately distracts and cleans up, so it feels stupidly consistent. Even when it’s nerfed, the vibe is basically “still busted, still the default.”

items.name: Heal Spirit
items.description: This little guy runs, jumps, and splashes just like his Spirit siblings, all for the same 1 Elixir cost. The difference? When he jumps onto your opponent's troops or buildings, he'll deal damage to them while healing your troops that are around him. However, he's weaker than his siblings when he doesn't have anything to heal.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Heal Spirit gets talked about like a lowkey game-changer: it’s a tiny cycle card that randomly flips fights and wins games when it connects right. People frame it as way more impactful than it looks on paper—more than just “another spirit”—because the heal swing can be disgusting value for 1 elixir. Compared to the other spirits it’s not always called the outright best, but it’s definitely treated as a sleeper pick that feels amazing when it clutches.

items.name: Giant Snowball
items.description: Is a push too hot to handle? A giant, 2 Elixir snowball will make them slow down for 3 seconds and chill out. As a side note, squishier troops tend to go splat while all but the heaviest of troops get knocked back. The Giant Snowball excels as a cheap, versatile disrupter.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Snowball gets talked about as one of those low-drama, always-useful spells: it stalls, knocks stuff around, and can be used on offense to steal hits and flip interactions. People don’t hype it as “broken,” but they do treat it like a super clean utility pick that quietly wins moments over and over. The base version is seen as limited compared to heavier spells (and it can’t do everything), but it’s still a really comfortable, consistent tool to have.

items.name: Royal Delivery
items.description: I'm sorry, did anyone order pure defensive destruction? Purchase this package for the low low price of 3 Elixir and it will be delivered with a 2 second delay, dropping from the sky, taking out air and ground swarms alike within its radius. Once it hits the ground, a Royal Recruit pops out to finish off the rest. It is perfect for defense, however not for offense, as it can't be placed on the opponent's side! You know what they say, 3 seconds or it's free!
items.llm_vibe_summary: Royal Delivery gets talked about like a “how do you NOT get value?” spell: huge drop damage, cleans up a bunch of stuff, and then you still get a free body to stall and mess up pushes. People who play it think it’s insanely efficient and just feels good every time it lands. On the other side, it’s the kind of card that makes you tilt because it randomly deletes your support troop and ruins your timing, even if it’s not the most versatile spell offensively.

items.name: Void
items.description: Stare at the void, and the void stares back. For 3 Elixir, this black magic spell encroaches a black hole in the arena, that deals huge damage to troops inside its 3 evil strikes, dealing more damage the fewer troops there are inside of it. This will cripple single units, but be worse against 2-4 units, and even worse with 5+ units.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Void gets talked about like a high-risk, low-reward “fake Fireball/Arrows” that only pops off when it’s a surprise or your opponent misplays into it. When people know you have it, it starts feeling mad inconsistent—sometimes it snipes a key unit, other times it just does nothing and you wish it was a real spell. The vibe is basically: niche, kinda gimmicky, and way more matchup/knowledge-dependent than most players want in a spell slot.

items.name: Goblin Curse
items.description: Who is the Witch who sent a green, Goblin-turning spell to the Arena? for 2 Elixir, this spell deals low damage in its duration, but makes it up this by turning defeated troops in its cursed ring into Goblins. You better not become a Goblin yourself if you don't want to face bureaucracy, however.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Goblin Curse is talked about like a straight-up “steal the game” button: most of the time it’s not the most standard, plug-and-play spell, but it has that cheesy ceiling where one good use can just decide the whole match. The vibe around it is less “meta staple” and more “I’m here to troll and highroll,” which makes it feel fun and satisfying when it works. Overall perception is that its value is swingy and matchup-y, but the win-out-of-nowhere potential is what people remember.

items.name: Spirit Empress
items.description: The Spirit Empress comes into the arena obeying your desires. If the player has between 3 and 6 Elixir, then she will come by foot; dealing good damage at close range. But if the player has 6 or more elixir, then she will come on her flying dragon; attacking a bit slower but at longer range with more hitpoints. This makes her great at offense, as well as at cheap emergency defense.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Spirit Empress gets talked about like a sleeper meta pick: the air form behind a Lava push feels straight-up unkillable unless you can stall it out, and the ground form is quietly high DPS. The vibe is mostly “people aren’t using this enough,” with more respect than hate. It’s not framed as brain-dead—more like a strong, under-the-radar support card that randomly makes matchups feel unfair if you don’t have the right answers.

items.name: Vines
items.description: For 3 Elixir, you will get 3 crazy overgrown vines. These vines will target the healthiest targets on his radius, dealing good damage, while grounding any air units, all for 2.5 seconds. However, they are not so numerous, as they can only entangle up to 3 targets.
items.llm_vibe_summary: Vines gets talked about like a super high-utility “mini-freeze” control spell that randomly fixes a ton of interactions—pinning stuff, buying time, and making your ground units actually connect. People rate it as one of those clean, efficient spells that also snipes annoying backline units (Dark Goblin/Firecracker/Archers types) and can even help deal with air by forcing it into bad spots. Overall vibe is that it’s just a really solid meta tool that quietly gets way more value than you’d expect.

supportItems.name: Tower Princess
supportItems.description: The King decides to guard his secondary towers with his trusted daughter, because family always comes first. High hitpoints and quick but low damage is always nice as a secondary means of defense.
supportItems.llm_vibe_summary: Tower Princess gets talked about like one of those forever-relevant cards: she’s basically never “bad” and always finds a home when her style is viable. People don’t frame her as brain-dead—more like a consistent, reliable pick that rewards clean timing and positioning. The main vibe is that she’s a staple you’ll keep seeing, and when she’s strong she can feel pretty annoying to deal with.

supportItems.name: Cannoneer
supportItems.description: Blast Away! The Cannoneer is a tower troop that deals a lot more damage but at a lower hit speed. This makes him great against low health, low count units and tanks, but susceptible to swarms. And use noise reducers. If you're his ally, you won't be targeted, but your ears will not like all that blast.
supportItems.llm_vibe_summary: Cannoneer gets talked about like a lowkey menace when it’s actually allowed to do its job: the DPS is nasty and if it’s hitting what it’s supposed to hit, you feel like you just deleted pushes for cheap. People also treat it as kinda underrated/underplayed compared to the “default” defensive options, but still very legit when the meta’s tanky and you need raw damage. It’s not really a “toxic” card—more like an efficient, annoying-to-break-through defensive piece that feels super satisfying when it locks in and melts stuff.

supportItems.name: Dagger Duchess
supportItems.description: This fiery royal isn't one for dainty lobs. She pelts enemies with quick, stinging daggers, building up to a ferocious finale. She charges up a "dagger bar" over time. Once charged, she depletes the dagger bar, unleashing a sharp flurry of all her blades at a single target, dealing devastating damage. She's great against swarms and chipping away at tanks, but can be baited and struggles against single-target heavyweights. And make sure to call her a "Duchess". She envies the Princess.
supportItems.llm_vibe_summary: The Reddit community largely views the Dagger Duchess as controversial, with many players arguing she was overpowered at release and disrupted the game’s balance and established deck strategies. Some users describe her as a “fun-killer” that made certain archetypes—especially cycle decks—much harder to play effectively. However, a smaller group defends her as a strategic sidegrade rather than a broken mechanic, saying players simply needed to adapt their playstyle.

supportItems.name: Royal Chef
supportItems.description: This magnificent chef trades less health and hit speed with delicious pancakes approved by the one and only Mini P.E.K.K.A.! And these pancakes are so special, that they even LEVEL UP troops, making them harder, better, faster, and stronger. However, they do sadly take time to make, so he prioritizes healthy troops who hasn't gotten a pancake! Sharing is caring, after all. Troops whose hitpoints have been depleted to below 33% HP will not get the pancake, as well as buildings.
supportItems.llm_vibe_summary: The Royal Chef is widely seen on Reddit as a polarizing card, with many players arguing his mid-battle level-up mechanic is fundamentally unhealthy for game balance. Some users describe him as “toxic” or promoting low-skill strategies, while others complain about bugs and design issues that made him feel broken on release. However, a smaller segment of the community says the concept is creative and fun, even if it needed heavy balancing to fit the meta.
